messages.content:
What is a social role?
Question 2 options:

group's expectations regarding what is appropriate and acceptable for the thoughts and behavior of its members

group's knowledge about the sequence of events in a specific setting

person's knowledge about the sequence of events in a specific setting

a pattern of behavior that is expected of a person in a given setting or group
a pattern of behavior that is expected of a person in a given setting or group.

Which of the following strategies would effectively prevent groupthink from occurring?
Question 5 options:

avoiding the development of any contingency plans

having the group leader make their position known before discussion begins

seeking outside opinions on group decisions

voting publicly in front of group members
seeking outside opinions on group decisions.

Which of the following is the best example of the foot-in-the-door technique of persuasion?
Question 8 options:

anti-smoking advertisements that rely on celebrity spokespeople advocating for reduction in cigarette use

anti-smoking advertisements that use charts and graphs to show how many people die from smoking-related causes each year

getting your parents to agree to cut their smoking down by a few cigarettes, then asking them to quit altogether

supporting your sister who has decided to go cold turkey in order to quit smoking
getting your parents to agree to cut their smoking down by a few cigarettes, then asking them to quit altogether.

Provide an example of cognitive dissonance from your own experience. In your example, you must demonstrate your understanding of the cognitive dissonance so provide an explanation in your example.

 Respond to the following question in your response: Do you agree that cognitive dissonance can cause attitude change? Why or why not? Using the information found in your course reading or other sources, you must elaborate on this section.

One example of cognitive dissonance from my own experience was when I purchased an expensive dress for an event, but later realized that I overspent and should have been more responsible with my money. The cognitive dissonance occurred when I wanted to justify my purchase to myself and others, despite knowing deep down that it was an unnecessary expense. In order to cope with the dissonance, I found myself making excuses for the purchase, such as telling myself that I deserved to treat myself or that the event was a rare opportunity.

I agree that cognitive dissonance can cause attitude change. According to the course readings, cognitive dissonance is a state of mental discomfort that arises when a person holds two or more contradictory beliefs, attitudes, or values. This discomfort can lead people to seek out ways to reduce the dissonance, which can include changing their attitudes or behaviors. In my example, I experienced cognitive dissonance because my desire to justify my purchase conflicted with my knowledge that I should be more responsible with my money. In order to reduce the dissonance, I eventually changed my attitude towards the purchase by acknowledging that it was an unnecessary expense and resolving to be more responsible with my spending in the future. Overall, cognitive dissonance can be a powerful force in shaping attitudes and behaviors, as people strive to reduce the discomfort caused by conflicting beliefs or values.
add in text citations

One example of cognitive dissonance from my own experience was when I purchased an expensive dress for an event, but later realized that I overspent and should have been more responsible with my money (Festinger, 1957). The cognitive dissonance occurred when I wanted to justify my purchase to myself and others, despite knowing deep down that it was an unnecessary expense. In order to cope with the dissonance, I found myself making excuses for the purchase, such as telling myself that I deserved to treat myself or that the event was a rare opportunity.

I agree that cognitive dissonance can cause attitude change (Festinger, 1957). According to the course readings, cognitive dissonance is a state of mental discomfort that arises when a person holds two or more contradictory beliefs, attitudes, or values (Aronson et al., 2019). This discomfort can lead people to seek out ways to reduce the dissonance, which can include changing their attitudes or behaviors. In my example, I experienced cognitive dissonance because my desire to justify my purchase conflicted with my knowledge that I should be more responsible with my money. In order to reduce the dissonance, I eventually changed my attitude towards the purchase by acknowledging that it was an unnecessary expense and resolving to be more responsible with my spending in the future. Overall, cognitive dissonance can be a powerful force in shaping attitudes and behaviors, as people strive to reduce the discomfort caused by conflicting beliefs or values. 

References:
Aronson, E., Wilson, T. D., Akert, R. M., & Sommers, S. R. (2019). Social psychology (10th ed.). Boston, MA: Pearson.
Festinger, L. (1957). A theory of cognitive dissonance. Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press.

Which of the following questions do ethical people ask when they're concerned with viability?

 a
"Am I following the rules?"
 b
"What's the impact of my actions in the long term?"
 c
"How can I communicate truthfully?"
 d
"Am I accepting responsibility for all of my decisions?"
"What's the impact of my actions in the long term?"

Following your school's code of conduct is an example of which ethical principle?

 a
Viability
 b
Transparency
 c
Trust
 d
Rule of law
d. Rule of law

Behaving ethically means doing

 a
what benefits you the most
 b
the right thing
 c
what your friends do
 d
only what's legal
b. the right thing

Whitley's ethical beliefs are the same whether she's at work, at school, or hanging out with her friends.  Whitley follows

 a
ethical principles
 b
ethical consequences
 c
situational beliefs
 d
popular beliefs
a. ethical principles

What is one benefit of following ethical principles?

 a
They eliminate the need for decisions.
 b
They change often.
 c
They are restrictive.
 d
They simplify your decision-making process.
d. They simplify your decision-making process.

Which of the following is an ethical issue?

 a
Deciding whether to have french fries or a salad
 b
Deciding whether to lie to a friend or not
 c
Choosing which movie to see this weekend
 d
Choosing between buying a new shirt or a new book
b. Deciding whether to lie to a friend or not.

Ethical issues deal with _____________ choices.

 a
enjoyable and unpleasant
 b
right and wrong
 c
realistic and unrealistic
 d
beneficial and non-beneficial
b. Right and wrong.

Vivian told her parents she was going to the library to study when she was actually going to the movies with friends.  Her parents found out, and now they don't believe Vivian when she tells them where she's going.  Because of her behavior, Vivian is facing a(n)

 a
ethical principle.
 b
decision-making process.
 c
consequence.
 d
legal problem.
c. Consequence.

After Paul gets away with cheating on his Spanish homework, his friends start cheating on their homework, too.  This is an example of which consequence of unethical behavior?

 a
Legal problems
 b
Lost trust
 c
A poor reputation
 d
A bad example
d. A bad example.

When Layla is caught stealing from a department store, the court orders her to do community service.  Which consequence of unethical behavior is Layla facing?

 a
Lost trust
 b
Legal problems
 c
A bad example
 d
A poor reputation
b. Legal problems.

Integrity means acting with 

 a
humility.
 b
honesty.
 c
a sense of humor.
 d
leadership skills.
b. Honesty.

Ava doesn't agree with her classmate's opinion, but she listens carefully instead of starting an argument or insulting her.  Which ethical principle is Ava illustrating?

 a
Trust
 b
Transparency
 c
Integrity
 d
Respect
d. Respect.

How can you show respect for a person you're speaking with?

 a
Avoid listening to his/her opinions.
 b
Interrupt when you have a point.
 c
Be polite and maintain eye contact.
 d
Start an argument when you don't agree.
c. Be polite and maintain eye contact.

If one of your co-workers is treating another co-worker unfairly, what is the ethical thing to do?

 a
Be aggressive.
 b
Speak up.
 c
Ignore it.
 d
Look for another job.
b. Speak up.

You make sure everyone working on your group project has an equal share of the work because you care about the ethical principle of 

 a
accountability.
 b
rule of law.
 c
fairness.
 d
transparency.
c. Fairness.

When DeShawn's teacher compliments his work on his group project, he's sure to give credit to the other group members who helped.  DeShawn is showing

 a
transparency.
 b
viability.
 c
rule of law.
 d
trust.
a. Transparency.

Holden's group gets a low grade on a project because he didn't complete his share of the work.  He owns up to his mistake and apologizes.  Holden is demonstrating

 a
viability.
 b
rule of law.
 c
accountability.
 d
trust.
c. Accountability.

Responsibility is a key part of which ethical principle?

 a
Fairness
 b
Transparency
 c
Accountability
 d
Rule of law
c. Accountability.

Marc keeps his friend's secret instead of sharing it with the rest of his friends.  Which ethical principle is Marc illustrating?

 a
Viability
 b
Accountability
 c
Trust
 d
Transparency
c. Trust.

At work, a customer gives Shannon an extra $10.  Instead of pocketing the money, Shannon lets the customer know he overpaid.  Shannon is acting with 

 a
viability
 b
transparency.
 c
integrity
 d
rule of law.
c. Integrity.
